revision.text:
[[arz:ايتو تشوتا]][[bcl:Itō Chūta]][[de:Itō Chūta]][[en:Itō Chūta]][[fr:Itō Chūta]][[ja:伊東忠太]][[ko:이토 주타]][[zh:伊东忠太]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Itō Chūta (伊東忠太).jpg|birth_date={{birth date|1867|10|26}}|death_date={{death date|1954|4|7}}|birth_place={{ill|Yonezawa|qid=Q734378|1=Yonezawa, Yamagata}}|death_place={{ill|Bunkyo|qid=Q212713|1=Bunkyō}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|architectural historian|qid=Q11486702|1=Architectural historian}}; {{ill|architecture critic|qid=Q17391654|1=architecture critic}}; {{ill|architect|qid=Q42973|1=Architect}}; {{ill|historian|qid=Q201788|1=Historian}}; {{ill|university teacher|qid=Q1622272|1=university teacher}}|awards={{ill|Order of Culture|qid=Q530124|1=Order of Culture}}; {{ill|Person of Cultural Merit|qid=Q2545449|1=Person of Cultural Merit}}|notable_works={{ill|Chōsen Shrine|qid=Q488935|1=Chōsen Shrine}}; {{ill|Haiseiden|qid=Q11386399|1=Haiseiden}}; {{ill|Hōkokubyō|qid=Q48744067|1=Hōkokubyō}}; {{ill|Karafuto Shrine|qid=Q11543654|1=Karafuto Shrine}}; {{ill|Kashihara Shrine|qid=Q710333|1=Kashihara Shrine}}; {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|qid=Q704686|1=Miyazaki Jingū}}; {{ill|Okura Museum of Art|qid=Q3329598|1=Okura Museum of Art}}; {{ill|Q64989690|qid=Q64989690|1=Q64989690}}; {{ill|Tsukiji Hongan-ji|qid=Q943255|1=Tsukiji Hongan-ji}}; {{ill|Uesugi Shrine|qid=Q10867478|1=Uesugi Shrine}}; {{ill|Yushima Seidō|qid=Q1760591|1=Yushima Seidō}}; {{ill|Yūshūkan|qid=Q2855971|1=Yūshūkan}}|native_name=伊東忠太|resting_place={{ill|Sōji-ji|qid=Q1250321|1=Sōji-ji}}}} {{Infobox architect |name = Itō Chūta
伊東忠太 |image = Itō Chūta (伊東忠太).jpg |image_size = |caption = Pictured in May 1954 edition of {{nihongo|''International Architecture''|国際建築|Kokusai kenchiku}} |nationality ={{JPN}} |birth_date = 26 October 1867 |birth_place = {{ill|Yonezawa, Yamagata|en|Yonezawa, Yamagata|qid=Q734378|lt=Yonezawa, Yamagata|1=Yonezawa, Yamagata}} |death_date = 7 April 1954 |death_place = [[Bunkyō, Tokyo]] |alma_mater = {{ill|University of Tokyo|en|University of Tokyo|qid=Q7842|lt=Imperial University|1=University of Tokyo}} |practice = [[Japan Art Academy]] (final) |significant_buildings = Tokyō University of Commerce
{{ill|Tsukiji Hongan-ji|en|Tsukiji Hongan-ji|qid=Q943255|lt=Tsukiji Hongan-ji|1=Tsukiji Hongan-ji}}
{{ill|Hitotsubashi University|en|Hitotsubashi University|qid=Q204626|lt=Kanematsu Auditorium at Hitobashi University|1=Hitotsubashi University}} |significant_projects = |significant_design = |awards = [[Order of the Sacred Treasure]] {{ill|Order of Culture|en|Order of Culture|qid=Q530124|lt=Order of Culture|1=Order of Culture}} }} {{family name hatnote|Itō|lang=Japanese}} {{nihongo|'''Itō Chūta'''|伊東 忠太|extra=26 October 1867 – 7 April 1954}} was a Japanese {{ill|Architect|en|Architect|qid=Q42973|lt=Architect|1=Architect}}, {{ill|Architectural historian|en|Architectural historian|qid=Q11486702|lt=Architectural historian|1=Architectural historian}}, and {{ill|Critic|en|Critic|qid=Q6430706|lt=Critic|1=Critic}}. He is recognized as the leading architect and architectural theorist of early 20th-century [[:en:Imperial Japan|Imperial Japan]].{{cite book |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sOJX4j533JoC&q=ito+chuta+imperial&pg=PA240 |title=Challenging past and present: the metamorphosis of 19th-century Japanese art |editor=Conant, Ellen P |year=2006 |publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]] |author=Watanabe Toshio |chapter=Japanese Imperial Architecture: from Thomas Roger Smith to Itō Chūta |pages=240–253 |isbn=978-0-8248-2937-7}} ==Biography== Second son of a doctor in {{ill|Yonezawa, Yamagata|en|Yonezawa, Yamagata|qid=Q734378|lt=Yonezawa|1=Yonezawa, Yamagata}}, present-day {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}}, Itō was educated in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}.{{cite news |url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/text/fa20030423a2.html |title=Chuta Ito: A builder of dreams |author=Tai Kawabata |work={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|qid=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}} |date=23 April 2003 |accessdate=24 February 2012}} From 1889 to 1892 he studied under {{ill|Tatsuno Kingo|en|Tatsuno Kingo|qid=Q1186865|lt=Tatsuno Kingo|1=Tatsuno Kingo}} in the Department of Architecture at the {{ill|University of Tokyo|en|University of Tokyo|qid=Q7842|lt=Imperial University|1=University of Tokyo}}. {{ill|Josiah Conder (architect)|en|Josiah Conder (architect)|qid=Q1187770|lt=Josiah Conder|1=Josiah Conder (architect)}} was still teaching in the department, while {{ill|Ernest Fenollosa|en|Ernest Fenollosa|qid=Q933253|lt=Ernest Fenollosa|1=Ernest Fenollosa}} and {{ill|Okakura Kakuzō|en|Okakura Kakuzō|qid=Q942995|lt=Okakura Kakuzō|1=Okakura Kakuzō}} were also influential in the formation of Itō's ideas.{{cite journal |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110004136822 |title=A Study on Chuta Itoh's architectural idea: influence on Chuta Itoh's artistic idea of E. F. Fenollosa and Tensin Okakura |author=Suzuki Yuichi |journal=Summaries of Technical Papers of Annual Convention |publisher=[[Architectural Institute of Japan]] |year=1984 |volume=59 |language=Japanese |pages=2703–4}} For graduation he designed a {{ill|Gothic architecture|en|Gothic architecture|qid=Q176483|lt=Gothic cathedral|1=Gothic architecture}} and wrote a dissertation on architectural theory. His doctoral thesis was on the architecture of [[Hōryū-ji]].{{cite book |title=Meiji Revisited: The Sites of Victorian Japan |author=Finn, Dallas |publisher=[[Weatherhill]] |year=1995 |isbn=0-8348-0288-0 |pages=167f}} He was professor of architecture at the Imperial University from 1905, then of {{ill|Waseda University|en|Waseda University|qid=Q274486|lt=Waseda University|1=Waseda University}} from 1928.{{cite web |url=http://www.tobunken.go.jp/japanese/bukko/1954.html |title=伊東忠太 (建) 昭和29th year 4月7日没 |publisher=[[National Research Institute for Cultural Properties, Tokyo]] |accessdate=24 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20121019223800/http://www.tobunken.go.jp/japanese/bukko/1954.html |archivedate=19 October 2012 }} Itō travelled widely, to the {{ill|Forbidden City|en|Forbidden City|qid=Q80290|lt=Forbidden City|1=Forbidden City}} with photographer {{ill|Ogawa Kazumasa|en|Ogawa Kazumasa|qid=Q3133048|lt=Ogawa Kazumasa|1=Ogawa Kazumasa}} in 1901 and subsequently, after fourteen months in China, to Burma, India, Sri Lanka, Turkey, Europe and the United States.{{cite web |url=http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=679&lang=en |title=Scenes from Late Qing Dynasty China: Photographs by Ogawa Kazumasa, Hayasaki Kokichi and Sekino Tadashi |publisher=[[Tokyo National Museum]] |accessdate=25 February 2012}} Later he was involved in the planning of [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] in {{ill|Seoul|en|Seoul|qid=Q8684|lt=Seoul|1=Seoul}} and a survey of the monuments of {{ill|Rehe Province|en|Rehe Province|qid=Q1148671|lt=Rehe|1=Rehe Province}} in {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|qid=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo|1=Manchukuo}}.{{cite journal |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110004655491 |title=Selection of the site for the Chōsen Shrine 1912–1918: Its relations to development of Japanese settlement and the early urban improvement in Keijo (Seoul) |language=Japanese |author=Aoi Akihito |journal=Journal of Architecture, Planning and Environmental Engineering. Transactions of AIJ |publisher=[[Kobe Design University]] |year=1999 |volume=521 |pages=211–8}}{{cite journal |url=http://ci.nii.ac.jp/naid/110004658389 |title=The investigation and preservation activities of the heritage of Jehol in Manchukuo: Cross-cultural understanding through the investigation and preservation activities of historical buildings in Japanese colony |author=Tanaka Sadahiko |journal= Journal of Architecture, Planning and Environmental Engineering |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese |year=2003 |volume=68 |issue=569 |pages=201–8|doi=10.3130/aija.68.201_3 |doi-access=free }} He incorporated elements of the {{ill|History of architecture|en|History of architecture|qid=Q8180985|lt=diverse architectural styles|1=History of architecture}} he encountered in his many writings and approximately one hundred design projects.{{cite web |url=http://www.lib.pref.yamagata.jp/kenjin/itou.html |title=伊東忠太 |publisher={{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} |accessdate=25 February 2012 |archive-date=3 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303204449/http://www.lib.pref.yamagata.jp/kenjin/itou.html |url-status=dead }} He was also a leading proponent of the [[:en:Imperial Crown style|Imperial Crown style]] of architecture, which had been developed for the Japanese Empire by architect {{ill|Shimoda Kikutaro|en|Shimoda Kikutaro|qid=Q28784318|lt=Shimoda Kikutaro|1=Shimoda Kikutaro}}.{{cite book|author=Alistair Fair|title=Setting the Scene: Perspectives on Twentieth-Century Theatre Architecture|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=iQOrCwAAQBAJ&pg=PT101|date=3 March 2016|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-317-05691-1|pages=101–}}{{cite book|author=Francis Chia-Hui Lin|title=Heteroglossic Asia: The Transformation of Urban Taiwan|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BYIcBgAAQBAJ&pg=PT85|date=9 January 2015|publisher=Taylor & Francis|isbn=978-1-317-62637-4|pages=85–}} Itō helped formulate the [[Cultural Properties of Japan#1897 Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law|Ancient Temples and Shrines Preservation Law]] of 1897, an early measure to protect the {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q858308|lt=Cultural Property (Japan)|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}}.{{cite book |title=Architecture and Authority in Japan |last=Coaldrake |first=William Howard |year=1996 |publisher=[[Routledge]] |isbn=0-415-05754-X |page=248}} He is also credited with {{ill|Neologism|en|Neologism|qid=Q130989|lt=coining|1=Neologism}} the {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}} term for architecture, namely {{nihongo|''kenchiku''|建築}} (''lit''. 'erection of buildings') in place of the former {{nihongo|''zōkagaku''|造家学}} (''lit.'' 'study of making houses'). A member of the {{ill|Japan Academy|en|Japan Academy|qid=Q1683229|lt=Japan Academy|1=Japan Academy}}, in 1943 he was awarded the {{ill|Order of Culture|en|Order of Culture|qid=Q530124|lt=Order of Culture|1=Order of Culture}}. Itō has more recently been criticised, with specific reference to his writings on [[Ise Grand Shrine]], for having 'blurred a religio-political discourse with an architectural discourse'.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0eGd7_zKgLUC&q=ito+chuta+ise&pg=PA66 |title=Kenzo Tange and the Metabolist movement: urban utopias of modern Japan |author=Zhongjie Lin |publisher=[[Routledge]] |year=2010 |page=67 (quoting Jonathan M. Reynolds) |isbn=978-0-415-77659-2}} ==Projects== {| class="wikitable sortable" style="width:100%; background:#fff;" |- !width="30%" align="left" style="background:#ffdead;" | Project !width="10%" align="left" style="background:#ffdead;" | Date !width="10%" align="left" style="background:#ffdead;" | Location !width="30%" align="left" style="background:#ffdead;" class="unsortable"| Comments !width="20%" align="left" style="background:#ffdead;" class="unsortable"| image |- | [[:en:Heian Jingū|Heian Jingū]]{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/koho/eng/preview/24.html |title=Heian-jingu Shrine |publisher={{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}} |accessdate=24 February 2012}} || 1895 || {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} || recreation on a smaller scale of the Daikokuden (Great Hall of State) of the ancient capital of {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}; Itō worked with fellow architect Kiko Kiyoyoshi, drawing on his studies of old records and {{ill|Emakimono|en|Emakimono|qid=Q1190781|lt=picture scrolls|1=Emakimono}} || [[File:Heian Jingu under construction.JPG|250px]] |- | {{nihongo|{{ill|Asano Sōichirō|en|Asano Sōichirō|qid=Q4803533|lt=Asano Sōichirō|1=Asano Sōichirō}} pavilion|浅野総一郎邸}}{{cite book |title=Meiji Revisited: The Sites of Victorian Japan |author=Finn, Dallas |publisher=[[Weatherhill]] |year=1995 |isbn=0-8348-0288-0 |page=191}} || 1909 || {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} || Japanese-style pavilion; destroyed in the [[:en:Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]] || |- | {{nihongo|Niraku Villa|[[:ja:二楽荘|二楽荘]]|Nirakusō}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/library/bunkodayori/hon_31.html |title=大谷光瑞と二楽荘 |publisher={{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|qid=Q48320|lt=Kobe City|1=Kobe}} |accessdate=25 February 2012 |archive-date=3 March 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303231932/http://www.city.kobe.lg.jp/information/institution/institution/library/bunkodayori/hon_31.html |url-status=dead }} || 1910 || {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|qid=Q48320|lt=Kobe|1=Kobe}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}} || for {{ill|Ōtani Kōzui|en|Ōtani Kōzui|qid=Q386254|lt=Ōtani Kōzui|1=Ōtani Kōzui}}, one of the pioneering explorers of {{ill|Central Asia|en|Central Asia|qid=Q27275|lt=Central Asia|1=Central Asia}} and the {{ill|Silk Road|en|Silk Road|qid=Q36288|lt=Silk Road|1=Silk Road}}; destroyed by arson on 18 October 1932; to the north of {{ill|Konan University|en|Konan University|qid=Q1037145|lt=Konan University|1=Konan University}}; photographic documentation exists || |- | Asoka Shinryōjo{{cite book |title=Meiji Revisited: The Sites of Victorian Japan |author=Finn, Dallas |publisher=[[Weatherhill]] |year=1995 |isbn=0-8348-0288-0 |pages=200f}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.kyoto.jp/bunshi/bunkazai/siteisyasinn/kennsetumei02.htm#Anchor-35477 |title=京都市指定・登録文化財－建造物 – 本願寺伝道院 |publisher={{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}} |accessdate=24 February 2012}} || 1912 || {{ill|Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1077022|lt=Shimogyō-ku|1=Shimogyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} || for the [[Jōdo Shinshū|Shinshū]] Believers Life Insurance Company; now the Hongan-ji Dendo'in; {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q858308|lt=Municipal Cultural Property|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}} || {{coord|34|59|28.9|N|135|45|14.3|E|display=inline|type:landmark_region:JP_scale:10000}} |- | {{nihongo|Main Gate|正門|Seimon}}, [[:en:Tokyo Imperial University|Tokyo Imperial University]]{{cite book |title=Meiji Revisited: The Sites of Victorian Japan |author=Finn, Dallas |publisher=[[Weatherhill]] |year=1995 |isbn=0-8348-0288-0 |pages=242f}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00000815 |title=東京大学本郷正門及び門衛所 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=24 February 2012}} || 1912 || [[:en:Bunkyo, Tokyo|Bunkyō]], {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} || replacement for the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo-period|1=Edo period}} Akamon, moved to one side; {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} was the first to ride through, on graduation day 1912; Itō was professor at the University from 1905; [[Cultural Properties of Japan#categories of registered Cultural Properties|Registered Tangible Cultural Property]] || [[File:University of Tokyo - Hongo campus main gate.JPG|250px]] |- | [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Jingū]] || 1920 || [[:en:Shibuya, Tokyo|Shibuya]], {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} ||shrine to {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}}; destroyed in the {{ill|Bombing of Tokyo|en|Bombing of Tokyo|qid=Q59886|lt=Tokyo air raids of World War II|1=Bombing of Tokyo}}; rebuilt in 1958 following the original design || [[File:Hitlerjugend visit to Meiji Shrine 1938.jpg|250px]] |- | {{nihongo|Uesugi Jinja|[[:ja:上杉神社|上杉神社]]}}{{cite web |url=http://www.city.yonezawa.yamagata.jp/1125.htm |title=上杉神社 |publisher={{ill|Yonezawa, Yamagata|en|Yonezawa, Yamagata|qid=Q734378|lt=Yonezawa City|1=Yonezawa, Yamagata}} |accessdate=25 February 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130318055919/http://www.city.yonezawa.yamagata.jp/1125.htm |archive-date=18 March 2013 |url-status=dead }} || 1923 || {{ill|Yonezawa, Yamagata|en|Yonezawa, Yamagata|qid=Q734378|lt=Yonezawa|1=Yonezawa, Yamagata}}, {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Yamagata Prefecture|1=Yamagata Prefecture}} || rebuilding after a great fire in 1919 that destroyed over a thousand buildings; in the city of Itō's birth || [[File:Uesugi shrine 1.jpg|250px]] |- | {{nihongo|Great Hall|大殿|Daiden}}, {{ill|Zōjō-ji|en|Zōjō-ji|qid=Q249139|lt=Zōjō-ji|1=Zōjō-ji}}{{cite web |url=http://www.zojoji.or.jp/history/index.html |title=増上寺の歴史 |publisher={{ill|Zōjō-ji|en|Zōjō-ji|qid=Q249139|lt=Zōjō-ji|1=Zōjō-ji}} |accessdate=25 February 2012 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20120222233538/http://www.zojoji.or.jp/history/index.html |archivedate=22 February 2012 }} || 1925 || {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|qid=Q190088|lt=Minato|1=Minato, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} || an earlier hall was lost in a fire in 1873 and its replacement in a fire in 1909; Itō's hall was destroyed in 1945; the Great Hall was rebuilt in 1978 || |- | {{nihongo|Tekigai Villa|荻外荘|Tekigaisō}} || 1927 || [[:en:Suginami, Tokyo|Suginami]], {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} || for {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=Prime Minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|qid=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe|1=Fumimaro Konoe}}, founder of the [[:en:Taisei Yokusankai|Taisei Yokusankai]] movement || [[File:Tekigai-so.jpg|250px]] |- | {{nihongo|Gion Kaku|祇園閣}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00000413 |title=祇園閣 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=24 February 2012}} || 1927 || {{ill|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|en|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1203862|lt=Higashiyama-ku|1=Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} || 34 m; in [[Gion]]; [[Cultural Properties of Japan#categories of registered Cultural Properties|Registered Tangible Cultural Property]] || [[File:Gionkaku.jpg|250px]] |- | {{nihongo|Ōkura Shūkokan|[[:ja:大倉集古館|大倉集古館]]}}{{cite book |title=The Architecture of Tōkyō |author=Watanabe Hiroshi |publisher=Edition Axel Menges |year=2001 |isbn=3-930698-93-5}}{{cite web |url=http://www.shukokan.org/english/index.html |title=Okura Museum of Art – outline |publisher=Okura Museum of Art |accessdate=24 February 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101127151353/http://shukokan.org/english/index.html |archive-date=27 November 2010 |url-status=dead }}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00000630 |title=大倉集古館陳列館 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=24 February 2012}} || 1927 || {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|qid=Q190088|lt=Minato|1=Minato, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} || rebuilding after the [[:en:Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]]; houses the [[:en:Ōkura Museum of Art|Ōkura Museum of Art]] with a collection that includes three {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}; Registered Tangible Cultural Property || [[File:Okura Shukokan 01.jpg|250px]] |- | {{nihongo|Kanematsu Auditorium|兼松講堂|Kanematsu kōdō}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00001765 |title=一橋大学兼松講堂 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=24 February 2012}} || 1927 || {{ill|Kunitachi, Tokyo|en|Kunitachi, Tokyo|qid=Q330007|lt=Kunitachi|1=Kunitachi, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} || {{ill|Romanesque Revival architecture|en|Romanesque Revival architecture|qid=Q744373|lt=Romanesque Revival|1=Romanesque Revival architecture}} style; part of {{ill|Hitotsubashi University|en|Hitotsubashi University|qid=Q204626|lt=Hitotsubashi University|1=Hitotsubashi University}}; Registered Tangible Cultural Property || [[File:Kanematsu auditorium.jpg|250px]] |- | {{nihongo|Former [[Hankyu Umeda Station#Hankyu Railway|Hankyū Umeda Station Concourse]]|旧阪急梅田駅地上駅コンコース}}{{cite web |url=http://rail.hankyu.co.jp/eehagaki/search_by_area/osaka_toyonaka/ |title=旧梅田駅コンコース |publisher=[[Hankyu Railway]] |accessdate=25 February 2012 }}{{Dead link|date=February 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} || 1929 || {{ill|Kita-ku, Osaka|en|Kita-ku, Osaka|qid=Q1207746|lt=Kita-ku|1=Kita-ku, Osaka}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}} || with dome, gilding, chandeliers, and [[:en:Arabesque (Islamic art)|arabesque]] || [[File:Hankyu Umeda Osaka Japan June 2005 A.jpg|250px]] |- | {{nihongo|Tokyo Memorial Hall|[[:ja:東京都慰霊堂|東京都慰霊堂]]|Tōkyōto ireidō}}{{cite web |url=http://www.tokyoireikyoukai.or.jp/ireido.html |title=東京都慰霊堂 |publisher=Tokyo Memorial Association |accessdate=24 February 2012}} || 1930 || {{ill|Sumida, Tokyo|en|Sumida, Tokyo|qid=Q235135|lt=Sumida|1=Sumida, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} || Dedicated to 58,000 victims of the [[:en:Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]] of 1 September 1923 and 105,000 victims of the {{ill|Bombing of Tokyo|en|Bombing of Tokyo|qid=Q59886|lt=Bombing of Tokyo|1=Bombing of Tokyo}} on the night of 9/10 March 1945 || [[File:The Great Kanto Earthquake. at Tokyo Memorial Hall for the Casualties.jpg|250px]] |- | {{nihongo|Tokyo Reconstruction Memorial Hall|東京都復興記念館|Tōkyōto fukkō kinenkan}} || 1931 || {{ill|Sumida, Tokyo|en|Sumida, Tokyo|qid=Q235135|lt=Sumida|1=Sumida, Tokyo}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} || houses exhibits relating to reconstruction after the Great Kantō earthquake; located in [[:en:Yokoamicho Park|Yokoamichō Park]] near the Tokyo Memorial Hall || [[File:Hukko kinenkan.jpg|250px]] |- | {{ill|Yūshūkan|en|Yūshūkan|qid=Q2855971|lt=Yūshūkan|1=Yūshūkan}} || 1931 || {{ill|Chiyoda, Tokyo|en|Chiyoda, Tokyo|qid=Q214051|lt=Chiyoda|1=Chiyoda, Tokyo}}, {{nihongo|Shōgyōden|聖教殿}}, {{ill|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|en|Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|qid=Q786113|lt=Hokekyō-ji|1=Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)}} || 1931 || {{ill|Ichikawa, Chiba|en|Ichikawa, treasures, including texts by
